replies.text: Mike Holdom basically you can use anything that is conductive - some metals will plate on and some won't - you find a metal that will plate the over plate that - I am not sure about 'off the shelf' magnetic paint as it uses iron oxide - but it is worth an experiement i would guess

text:

These are at a length of approx 1 metre. I can make a long bath out of half of a PVC pipe blocked at each end,  dc power supplies with voltage set or current limiting are no problem as I have built several.
Regards Robert Scott

author: @ldanielrosa
text:
I'm intrigued by a general purpose electroplating solution.  Granted it can't be as efficient for some items as the specialized baths, but it's far less toxic than some.

I'm curious if this will work for lead or silver, or if the insoluble sulfates will precipitate.

I'm also curious about using this for electrowinning, and if the insoluble sulfates can be used to the advantage in the separation (anode slime).

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Now that's kind of cool. However for you it's a bit of a pain right now as just about the only way to remove the resulting oxide to clean metal is to sand blast it!

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hiya mate, Yeah that is interesting. I'm afraid rust removal with epsom salt will do this - a better alternative is sodium carbonate as the salt. Epsom salts and steel actually have a reverse potential when the charge is removed - you can make your own homemade rechargeable battery this way -you fill a cell with two steel plates and a solution of epsom salt put a charger on it for a few minutes and the take it off charge - attach an LED and the LED will light up.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: citric acid is a tricarboxylic acid

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: i'm sorry it's been a little while since i looked at this. I have kind of got caught up in the graphene research i am doing - i will get back to this - just not sure when. When i do get back to it - the power supply is the thing i am going to work on. You're right of course acetic acid is a carboxylic acid. But oxalic acid is dicarboxylic and that is going to have quite an effect. If it was me - i would just try it and see  - but i am not sure how good it will be. Cheers.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: i'm not worried about violating a patent - i'm worried about youtube taking the video down for copyright infringement. This way the information is out there. But i like your comments - keep them coming

author: @mowog59
text:
Acetic acid could be substituted for oxalic acid because it is a carboxylic acid could it not?  that would give me vinegar, cleaning solution and fertilizer :)   You used citric acid but is that a carboxylic acid?

great videos -  thanks for posting them...  A video on voltage and currents for electoplating would be great! I've got a variable power supply with a current limiter but I don't get the same results as the 4.5 volt 300 mA transformer that came with a plating kit I bought.

author: @AmpLogic
text:
A successful, commonly used brush plating solution for nickel:
By weight:
Nickel Sulfate: 10% - 15%
Nickel Chloride: 2% - 3%
Boric Acid: 0.5% - 1%
Hydrogen Chloride - .001% - .002%
Water: ~85%

author: @AmpLogic
text:
OK...Here's a newsflash.....It's impossible to violate a patent, if one isn't using information from the patent TO MAKE A PROFIT!!!!!  Why in the world would you think that you would get into trouble for using info from a patent to do home electroplating?  
Just buy the oxalic acid already, and don't try to re-invent the wheel.  Sheesh.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: what are you using to activate? what current per square centimeter are you using? and can you send a pic of your results to my e-mail i'll have a look - rob.
